title: Body scan from feet to crown
subtitle: A ten-minute bedtime meditation for moving physical attention from the feet up through the body.
excerpt: A ten-minute body scan meditation for bedtime. The practice moves attention slowly from the feet to the top of the head, pausing at each area to notice and release held tension. Lie down to do it.
sourceNotes: Body scan meditation has multiple lineages. The version here draws on the secular mindfulness adaptations that became widespread through Jon Kabat-Zinn's Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) programme in the 1980s. The bedtime adaptation is shaped for the calm-the-body theme of Day 3 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use in bed before sleep, or on a mat just before getting into bed, when you want to move physical tension out of the body methodically.

content.text: A ten-minute body scan for bedtime. The practice moves attention slowly from the soles of the feet to the top of the head. Do it lying down.
New to meditation? Read
body-based meditation
first.
The practice
Setting up
Lie on your back. Arms at your sides or resting on your stomach. Eyes closed. Take three slow breaths to announce to yourself that you are stopping.
The scan

Bring your attention to the soles of your feet. Notice temperature: are they warm or cool? Notice pressure: the weight of them against the mattress. If there's tension, let it release with an exhale. No effort — just notice and breathe.
Move to the calves. Notice them. Are they held? Exhale slowly and let the muscles soften toward the mattress. Take two breaths here if you need.
Knees. Thighs. Hips. Each area: notice, breathe out, let go of any holding. The hip joints carry a lot from a day's sitting and standing. Take an extra breath there.
Belly. Chest. Notice the movement of breathing here. Ribs expanding and falling. Let the breath do what it needs to without adjusting it.
Shoulders. Let them drop. Arms, elbows, forearms, hands. Notice whether your hands are clenched. Uncurl the fingers if they are.
Neck. Jaw. Tongue. The jaw and tongue hold more tension than most people expect. Let the tongue drop to the floor of the mouth. Let the jaw be loose.
Forehead. Scalp. Crown of the head.
You have arrived at the top. The whole body is noticed. Let it rest where it is.
Coming back

If you are doing this before sleep, stay where you are and let sleep arrive. If you are doing it as a standalone practice and want to come back to full wakefulness, take three deep breaths and open your eyes slowly.
Where this practice comes from

Body scan meditation draws on a long contemplative lineage. The secular adaptation used widely today traces through Jon Kabat-Zinn's Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR) programme, developed at the University of Massachusetts in the 1980s. The bedtime version here is shaped for the calm-the-body theme of Day 3 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
